title: Phegopteris gymnogrammoides
intro: Phegopteris gymnogrammoides, also known as maidenhair fern, is a type of fern that belongs to the family Dryopteridaceae. This plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory problems, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of P. gymnogrammoides include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, P. gymnogrammoides is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and can also be found in the form of capsules or extracts, often used to treat colds, flu, and other respiratory infections.
taxonomy: Phegopteris gymnogrammoides is classified as a species within the genus Phegopteris, which is a group of ferns in the family Dryopteridaceae. This classification is based on its morphological characteristics and genetic relationships with other species. The genus Phegopteris is further grouped into the order Polypodiales and class Polypodiopsida. The family Dryopteridaceae is part of the larger group of ferns known as Polypodiophyta. This classification helps to understand the evolutionary relationships and diversity within the plant kingdom.

constituents: Phegopteris gymnogrammoides contains various active constituents, including flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids. These compounds have been found to exhibit antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. The flavonoids present in Phegopteris gymnogrammoides have been identified as kaempferol, quercetin, and isorhapontigenin. The plant's alkaloid content has been reported to include rhaponticin and isorhapontigenin.
